sentences.text: Yeah.
Yeah.
They came to the office.
And they always told me what's going on.
I had also in the main office, a man who gave me the underground newspaper.
The underground newspaper, from the top to the bottom, you can spell a Polish newspaper.
And they said, and the sun is closer than the war, the Germans will be defeat.
Said that thing.
And so one day, I took the paper.
And I was reading.
I put the paper in the drawer.
And my, my desk was against the door.
And I asked the people who works their close, where you-- the Herring is coming back, knock the window and I will stop.
What I do, I will stop doing it.
So they didn't do it.
They didn't see them or something.
And he opened the door.
And he said, what do you have in the drawer?
I said, nothing.
He said, what do you have in the drawer?
I said, I have money.
And I took out fives zlotys or something.
And I gave it to him.
He shred it and put on my head.
And I said to myself, oh, god.
I'm not going to take anymore newspaper, underground.
Because it start the one-- they're going to kill me, it's OK.
But they are going to put me in such a terrible-- to tell me from where I have this newspaper.
Maybe I would have to tell.
So I took the newspaper.
I go back And I told them, don't give me any more.
I told him the story what happened.
If you did that job like that, I will give it to you more.
This was one of the-- of a way that they were in the underground, yes, this man.
Then I have five men who were working-- Polish people working on the underground.
And they went to-- they were very good to me.
They want to me whatever they can.
And they-- one day, they came over.
And they said, Dora, I brought some onions.
Would you like to draw-- take out this thing and leave me only the-- what do you call-- only the top around that.
I didn't ask you for what do you need it or something.
OK, I will do it.
First of all, I have the onions.
Can take it back home.
And they put inside ammunition inside, you know?
Did you see them do this?
I didn't see them.
But they, they see them.
Later, I learned after the war.
They told me what they did.
And this was not in our department.
This was in a different department where they have those ammunition to three centimeters.
They were big one.
It goes to the-- what do you call-- to the machine guns, you know, and this for the underground.
And the second time he came with another idea, to make him pouches, you know, to sew him pouches.
They were the thread and the needle, the sewing.
I did it.
And I gave it to them.
They put in between the legs.
And they cannot-- you know, when they, they check him out.
And so after the war, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I got married to my husband, we went to a, a restaurant there to call somebody from the restaurant.
And there is one of these men.
And he said, Dora, what are you doing here?
I said, I'm getting married.
This is my future husband.
Look at him.
And he said, where is the wedding?
I said, at Christmas, you know?
And he said, they have music?
I said, no.
I don't need music.
But I want to play on your wedding for free.
Where is the rest of you?
Ah, the rest is-- about two others, they came, too.
And they have the music.
And they learn how to play a Jewish music, something like that.
I was amazed with them.
Going back the-- to my sadist, Herring, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we had to have make the norm every day so many thousands of, of ammunition we had to get out.
If we cannot, if for example the-- some tools were broken or something, they beat up the man who has nothing to do with it was broken.
This was broken.
They didn't have the tools.
They didn't have enough, anything of anything.
So you have to have a, a special card to go to the main office to take it, the thing, you know?
And I write his name, my-- To take what?
To take the, the, the tools, what broken.
Before the-- he says that the machine is not working.
And I use to bring those things back.
And I write his name.
You can do everything what you want when you are in need.
[INAUDIBLE].
No.
It was here.
It was the music room. 1,500 people, they took to the music room on the top of the factory there.
It was a big, big thing.
They beat them up.
I was there.
I saw that.
[INAUDIBLE]
Huh?
Hasak. ?] [?
Hasak ?] is the name of the factory.
They have a lot of those factories in Poland, in Radom.
They have it [POLISH].
They have it in [POLISH].
They have it around everywhere.
And they grow up so fast.
And later on, they didn't have even materials.
They are taking old ammunition.
They brought them back and to renew it.
Those ammunition cannot even, you know, even-- what you call-- even-- they not able to do what they supposed to do.
From, from their front.
They have a lot of front in the east side.
The Germans used to come back already.
The-- it was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Austrian [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] man who was their engineer who works there.
And he used to buy the [INAUDIBLE].
He used to bring it to the office and leave it on my desk.
And I used to see that the, the road that they have to go back for strategic things, they have to go back from thing to thing.
The geography of Russia, I know exactly how it was, the geography.
They were almost close to Lublin.
It was very-- it Polish, in Poland.
So every time when something happened like that and I saw it-- in the German, newspaper I used to go around to the workers there.
And I told them, don't worry about.
I saw in the newspaper.
And the German, they're not going to tell me anything else because this is Krakow-- a newspaper from Krakow.
So what did you tell the workers?
That will make them a little feel that it's closer to the end, closer to the end, that 50% or more died-- more died.
They didn't survive.
Because they were younger, they were older.
The older people didn't survive, men especially.
Men were very-- they lost their, their will to live.
They didn't wash themselves.
They didn't try to shave.
They didn't have with what to shave, you know?
They, they were completely different than a woman.
I used to bring for the woman red paper to make her cheeks to look healthy.
They said take this and rub it around.
So when it was selection-- every month was a selection.
And they selected those lame, those very sick, and those who cannot work.
Where I work with them.
In the factory-- also, those people locked up already, you know, from other little state-- other little villages.
They took them over.
Or from [INAUDIBLE], they took from the Majdanek already.
There were non-Jews and Jews.
The non-Jews, they, they can go home.
They live in the home.
But they can-- they, they were-- what do you call in a factory-- the foremans.
They were foremans, you know?
They were foreman.
They used to work in the factory before the war.
And this was the only thing that they can survive not to be arrested or not to send to the-- to Germany, to another camp to-- where, you know, not Polish camp or German.
They don't speak German.
They don't know how to speak German.
It was very hard for them.
Yeah, I was there, too, at selection.
In the factory and out of the factory.
Not in the factory-- outside the factory.
They put the people around there.
They called names, numbers.
Not names-- they were number already.
They were have here numbers, for everyone who has to take-- have those numbers, like a cow.
It's here number.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah.
Why-- my Herring from this department.
From the other department, there was [?
Krauser. ?] And the other one was killed later.
The underground killed him.
It was-- I forgot his name already.
This was also Nazi.
Oh.
Where?
I was standing there, waiting for somebody to, to call me up, out.
And they never called me up.
Everyone has a number.
You don't work without a number.
I remember 1,500 people's numbers.
You tell me the number, I will tell you what is the name.
Anymore-- I think God gave you so many things to remember, so many things to, to accumulate during your life, that later on, you cannot remember.
Yeah.
Yeah.
We was walking home-- walking to the factory in the morning, very early in the morning.
And this [INAUDIBLE] boy was sitting.
And the-- a boy, yeah, the son.
The, the dogs are running after them.
I said, you run.
I stay I like dogs.
I like dogs.
I like animals.
I said, I will stay.
I like dogs.
I will talk to them.
I think that-- you have to wear this armband.
And I think they were taught, the, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the, the dogs train, trained to attack those people who wearing those armbands.
They [INAUDIBLE] you.
And they try to-- I, I close only my, my face.
And they bit me.
And so later on, I complain about that to whom?
To my commandant.
The next day, I didn't go to work.
They came in.
I told you, they, they grabbed me in my night gown and my robe to work.
And I work.
Nothing else I can do.
The commandant of the [INAUDIBLE].
His name was Hass.
He was very good to children.
And he killed older people.
What can you do?
One, and I work in the other one, the bigger one.
This was work [INAUDIBLE] B.
And the other one was work A.
B.
Yeah.
Yeah.
1944, 1944 in the beginning.
[INAUDIBLE]
I came to this work A-- B-- A.
And I went to work.
So I came there.
And I work at the machines in the beginning.
And later on, one of another-- my boss came over.
He asked me, what did you do in, in work B.
I said I was in the office.
He said, we need somebody-- this was Wagner.

It was military man, a engineer who was taking a-- he was, he was [INAUDIBLE] controlling the ammunition, what they take, what they buy, from-- this was Wehrmacht to buy from the SS for the factory, It belonged to them.
And he took me there to control-- to be a controller at the office.
Before that, he used to come to the office in work B.
He used to come to work B, to work A, to work C.
He used to come [INAUDIBLE].
And he used to leave me [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some little cards.
He saw there is that don't show you enemies that you are mad, like that.
Yeah.
I was scared of him like crazy.
I was-- I didn't know what to do.
But you can't believe a man in a, in a uniform to tell you something like that?
And I didn't react to that.
And one day, I said to him, tell me, what did you mean?
Why do you want from me, tell me stories like that?
He said, you have family in-- I was locked up already at the ammunition-- the work B.
And do you have family in the ghetto?
I said, yes.
He said, in 10 days is going to be a liquidation of the ghetto.
Don't tell anybody.
See?
And your obligation is to die together.
You cannot die every time in a life your family is taking away from you, you know?
So my father-- I don't know how he knew about that.
He came to the fence there to Hass, commandant of the lager.
And he ask about me.
And she's my daughter.
I would like to work.
He was very, very young, my father.
And he looks young all the time.
And he said, and I want you to come to the ghetto.
And I have something, nice thing for you in the ghetto.
He went there.
He took--
Hass.
He took some things-- we had a, a sewing machine in the house yet.
We had some paintings, Polish paintings, we had hidden away.
And he took those things.
He had some beautiful mother's robes, silk.
And he took this and some other things.
He also had a watch who my father received this from his father.
And his father received from his father for a bar mitzvah.
After bar mitzvah, he had those things.
This was a antique.
And He gave it to him.
No, I didn't see.
My father told me what they gave him.
My father was not a liar.
He told me.
And he is bribe.
And some Germans when they promise you they're going to do that, and they took money, and they cannot do it, they gave you back the money.
Can you imagine, another thing?
It's unbelievable what people can do.
When you had the opportunity to talk this people by yourself with nobody else as a witness, you can see what they really are and what, what make him do it what they do.
It was another [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] German who was the boss's assistant. he-- his name, I forgot.
I don't remember.
But he told me at night, specially work in the office at night, God forbid at night to work in a office.
It's terrible.
You are sleepy.
You are hungry.
And he came in.
And he said-- I ask him, what did you do during-- before the war?
He said, I was a boy who didn't have a job.
Well, finally, when Hitler came to power, he gave me a job.
And I was building-- not.
You were building?
You are architect?
He said, no.
I was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] helping the, the one who build the houses, the workers.
What do you call them?
You mean a carpenter?
Brick layers to bring the bricks up there.
And I said, how did you communicate with them?
And he start screaming.
They called him by name, schnell, schnell, schnell, you know?
And I said to him, you know, what are you going to do now?
It was so-- the end of-- on the end.
It was the end.
He said, everything is crazy, you see?
You're crazy.
Everything is crazy.
I said, now you tell me it's crazy.
Why didn't you tell me about three years ago?
Ah, three years ago, it was terrible.
You couldn't tell you, couldn't tell you, see, different kind of thing.
The third kind of-- it was meanwhile, I was very sick.
I have dysentery, terrible dysentery.
In work A.
Yeah, the second factory.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was so strong that when I had to go to the factory, I didn't go to the bathroom at all.
I have something there.
And I was bleeding.
I was a, a, a skeleton completely.
So the [?
Rosenbergers, ?] [?
Rosenbergers, ?] [?
Rosenbergers, ?] they were people who had to do-- the man had to do with the gardens, you know?
Yeah.
And he delivered the gardens to the casinos around there, and to the restaurants for the German officers.
And this is everything for the Germans, the big shots there.
And he was a very nice man.
I told you before that I have to go for the things what I-- for the, no.
No, forget it.
Not this.
So finally, I-- my mother said, maybe you can go.
My mother works there in the gardens there, my father, too.
But my father, if he would steal something, they will fight every time.
Because when you walk out-- that he had a hat-- and you said goodbye or-- he took it out.
And there is green beans, you know?
And they laughed there.
And they didn't take.
Who, who, who laughed?
The people who stood there-- the guards there.
Yeah.
They laughed when--
They laughed that it looked so comical that they took out.
And here, you have this green beans.
No, my mother told me.
And so my mother said, maybe you will go there.
And maybe you can eat something there.
And maybe you're going to be-- and so I told my Wagner that they have to go.
Because I'm sick.
I'm very sick.
And you don't know what's going on.
I'm so sick.
He said, do whatever you want to do.
And I went.
And [?
Rosenberger ?] took me.
And I was working in his office, you know, to this [INAUDIBLE] and this and this to went [INAUDIBLE] to this [INAUDIBLE].
One time, I go out in the fields.
And it was onions you know?
And I took out the onions from the ground, dirty.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't wash it, because I was afraid of going to wash it.
Somebody will see it.
They will take away from me.
That's it.
I start eating.
And then it was man.
He's standing in front of me, SS.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Fraulein, what are you doing here?
I said, eating.
And he says, why are you eating?
I'm hungry.
I'm eating.
And I said, why do you stay like that?
Here, he had S on this shirt.
I said, what are you doing?
He said, ah, I'm taking these prisoners there.
[INAUDIBLE] people, to the school, and there ask for them to be-- not liquidated-- to be shot down, shot down, you know?
I have-- [INAUDIBLE].
